metadata.title: State of AI in the Enterprise: The Untapped Edge
metadata.author: Deloitte AI Institute

content: Deloitte's 2026 State of AI in the Enterprise report, based on a survey of 3,235 director-to-C-suite leaders across 24 countries and 6 industries, maps where enterprises stand in AI adoption as of late 2025.
Core finding: AI access is expanding rapidly (up 50% YoY to ~60% of workers), but utilization, production deployment, and true business reimagination lag far behind access growth.

Only 34% of companies are using AI to deeply transform their business; 37% still use it at a surface level with no changes to underlying processes; and 84% have not redesigned jobs or workflows around AI capabilities.

Three macro trends—Sovereign AI (77% of firms weigh where AI is built), Agentic AI (74% plan deployment within 2 years but only 21% have mature governance), and Physical AI (58% already using it, 80% adoption projected within 2 years)—are reshaping enterprise AI strategy.

The report argues that the biggest opportunity lies not in experimentation but in translating pilots into scalable, measurable business value while keeping human judgment, governance, and purpose at the center.

content.point: Workforce AI access grew 50% in one year (from under 40% to ~60% of workers with sanctioned tools), yet fewer than 60% of those with access use AI in their daily workflow—meaning access is widening but utilization is stagnating.

Only 25% of organizations have moved 40%+ of AI experiments into production today, but 54% expect to reach that threshold within 3–6 months—highlighting a massive pending scale-up, if execution holds.

Revenue growth from AI remains largely aspirational: 74% of organizations hope to grow revenue through AI, but only 20% are currently doing so. Efficiency and cost reduction are the dominant realized benefits today.

Agentic AI governance is critically underdeveloped: nearly 3 in 4 companies plan to deploy autonomous AI agents within two years, yet only 1 in 5 (21%) report having a mature governance model for these agents.

Sovereign AI is a strategic reality, not just a policy issue: 77% of surveyed companies say the geographic location of AI development is a key factor when choosing new technologies, signaling that national and organizational technological autonomy now rivals innovation capability as a selection criterion.

content: Close the gap between access and activation: since fewer than 60% of workers with sanctioned AI tools use them daily, leaders should investigate behavioral and structural barriers to daily use and deploy targeted enablement programs—not just license provisioning.

Build governance before scaling agentic AI: given that only 21% of companies have mature oversight models for autonomous agents while 74% plan deployment within two years, organizations must develop agent governance frameworks, human-in-the-loop controls, and accountability structures before wide rollout.

Redesign work, not just skills: since 84% of companies have not redesigned jobs or workflows around AI, move beyond training employees and instead rearchitect roles, career paths, and processes to embed AI capabilities structurally—addressing the root cause of underutilization.

Establish a sovereign AI strategy: given that 77% of peers already weigh AI development location in vendor decisions, audit current technology supply chains for geographic dependencies and develop a position on where AI models and infrastructure should be developed and hosted.

Break the proof-of-concept trap by creating a clear pilot-to-production roadmap: as described by a surveyed healthcare AI leader, organizations without a coherent AI strategy suffer 'pilot fatigue.' Define upfront criteria for scaling successful pilots—including infrastructure, compliance, integration, and ROI measurement requirements.

Pursue strategic reinvention over efficiency optimization: since only 34% of companies are using AI to deeply reimagine their business models and the report explicitly warns that optimization of existing processes will not yield competitive differentiation, leadership should identify at least one business model or offering redesign initiative enabled by AI.

strengths: Large, globally diverse sample (3,235 senior leaders across 24 countries and 6 industries) gives the findings substantial credibility and cross-market relevance, avoiding single-market bias.

The report provides both quantitative benchmarks (specific percentages with year-over-year comparisons) and qualitative depth (15 C-suite interviews), making findings more grounded than survey-only reports.

The taxonomy of three emerging AI paradigms—Agentic, Physical, and Sovereign AI—is a useful and forward-looking framework that goes beyond generic GenAI discussion, giving practitioners concrete areas to plan against.

The 'proof-of-concept trap' section is one of the most practically useful passages: it honestly names the structural reasons pilots fail to reach production (infrastructure gaps, integration complexity, compliance overhead) rather than attributing failure to vague 'culture' issues.
weaknesses: The report is substantially truncated in the source provided (middle sections marked as omitted), meaning key sections—including the full Agentic AI, Physical AI, and AI preparedness findings—are not fully visible, limiting the depth of analysis available.

Survey responses are self-reported perceptions from senior leaders who may overestimate their organization's AI maturity; the report does not appear to cross-validate claims against independent performance data or third-party benchmarks.

The recommended actions (e.g., 'close the gap between access and activation,' 'build governance before scale') are strategically sound but remain at a high level of abstraction without specific implementation tools, decision frameworks, or prioritization criteria for practitioners.

The sample is skewed toward organizations that already have at least one working AI implementation—meaning findings may overrepresent AI-mature companies and understate the challenges facing organizations still at very early stages.
missing: ROI and financial return data: the report mentions that revenue growth is aspirational for 74% of firms, but does not provide quantified returns achieved by the 34% of deeply transforming companies to benchmark against.

Industry-specific breakdowns for most findings: while sectors like manufacturing and defense are mentioned in Physical AI context, most key statistics are reported at aggregate level without industry disaggregation.

Failure case studies or cautionary examples beyond the 'pilot fatigue' quote—concrete stories of what went wrong when governance or scaling was mishandled would strengthen prescriptive recommendations.

Methodology details on how 'maturity' levels (e.g., 'mature governance model') were defined and measured in the survey instrument, which affects interpretation of figures like the 21% agentic AI governance maturity claim.

thinking_framework.content: The report implicitly structures enterprise AI maturity along two intersecting axes: DEPTH OF TRANSFORMATION (surface-level efficiency → process redesign → full business reimagination) and BREADTH OF DEPLOYMENT (isolated pilots → selective production → enterprise-wide scaling). This produces a 3-tier taxonomy explicitly described in the report: Tier 1 (37%) — surface-level AI use with no change to underlying processes; Tier 2 (30%) — redesigning key processes around AI; Tier 3 (34%) — deeply transforming the business, reimagining business models, offerings, roles, and ways of working. Only Tier 3 organizations are described as achieving true competitive differentiation. The framework implies that most organizations are 'stuck' in Tiers 1–2 not because of technology limitations, but due to gaps in work redesign, governance build-out, and strategic coherence. A secondary implicit framework is the 'Access → Utilization → Value' pipeline: expanding access (60% of workers) does not automatically yield utilization (fewer than 60% of those use it daily), which does not automatically yield value (only 20% achieving revenue growth). Each stage requires distinct interventions.
thinking_framework.framework_name: AI Transformation Depth-Breadth Matrix + Access-to-Value Pipeline

knowledge_insights.content:
1. THE PILOT-PRODUCTION GAP IS STRUCTURAL, NOT MOTIVATIONAL: The report explicitly identifies why pilots stall—pilots run with small teams, clean data, and isolated environments, while production requires infrastructure investment, system integration, security reviews, compliance checks, monitoring, and ongoing maintenance. Use cases estimated at 3 months can stretch to 18 months when integration complexity emerges. This is a systems problem, not a talent or will problem.

2. CONFIDENCE IS RISING FASTER THAN CAPABILITY: 42% of companies believe their strategy is highly prepared for AI, and 30% say the same about risk and governance—both rising year-over-year—yet perceptions of preparedness declined for technical infrastructure, data management, and talent. Leaders feel more strategically ready than operationally ready, creating a dangerous overconfidence gap at scale.

3. SOVEREIGN AI IS A GEOPOLITICAL BUSINESS IMPERATIVE: The finding that 77% of companies factor AI development location into technology selection decisions signals that sovereign AI has crossed from government policy discourse into everyday enterprise procurement—meaning vendor selection is now partially a geopolitical risk management decision.

4. AGENTIC AI REPRESENTS A GOVERNANCE TIME BOMB: The combination of 74% of companies planning agentic AI deployment within 2 years alongside only 21% having mature governance for autonomous agents represents one of the most acute near-term enterprise risk scenarios described in the report. Autonomous agents that take consequential actions without adequate oversight structures represent a qualitatively different risk profile than passive GenAI tools.

5. PHYSICAL AI IS ALREADY MAINSTREAM, NOT EMERGING: With 58% of companies already using Physical AI (robotics, autonomous vehicles, drones) and 80% adoption projected within 2 years, this is no longer a future trend to monitor—it is a current operational reality that demands safety frameworks and human oversight protocols now, particularly in manufacturing, logistics, and defense.

content: PILOT FATIGUE QUOTE (Healthcare AI Leader): A healthcare AI leader interviewed for the report describes the consequences of AI strategy incoherence: 'If there is no coherent AI strategy in organizations, you are likely to see pilot fatigue. You're chasing the next shiny object, pressured to do something with AI without a real plan. I've seen many instances where people embark on pilots, but when asked how they'll scale up if successful, they often don't have an answer. Without a clear roadmap, executing a hundred pilots just leads to poor results and failed value creation.' This illustrates the structural problem of decentralized, vision-free AI experimentation.

SCALE ACCELERATION DATA POINT: The report notes that while only 25% of organizations have moved 40%+ of AI experiments into production today, 54% expect to reach that level within 3–6 months—used as evidence that the 'pathway to value is clear and achievable' for early scalers who have begun transitioning from pilot to enterprise mode.

PHYSICAL AI ADOPTION TRAJECTORY: The report cites that 58% of companies are already using Physical AI to some extent, with adoption projected to reach 80% within two years. Markets in Asia Pacific are cited as leading this adoption, driving integration of robotics, autonomous vehicles, and drones, with manufacturing, logistics, and defense sectors leading globally.

AI INVESTMENT SURGE: 84% of organizations are increasing their AI investments and 78% of leaders report greater confidence in the technology—cited alongside the finding that only 25% are reporting transformative business impact, illustrating the gap between investment sentiment and realized transformation.

UTILIZATION GAP: Even among workers with sanctioned AI tool access, fewer than 60% use AI in their daily workflow—and this pattern is largely unchanged from the prior year, suggesting behavioral adoption is not keeping pace with access provisioning.

WORK REDESIGN DEFICIT: 84% of companies have not redesigned jobs or the nature of work itself around AI capabilities. The report identifies this as a critical miss: organizations are training people on AI tools but not rearchitecting roles, workflows, and career paths to embed AI structurally.

TALENT AS THE BIGGEST BARRIER: Insufficient worker skills are identified as the biggest barrier to integrating AI into the business, yet fewer than half of companies are making significant adjustments to their talent strategies—creating a self-reinforcing constraint.

GOVERNANCE LAGGING DEPLOYMENT FOR AGENTIC AI: Only 21% of companies have a mature governance model for autonomous agents, even as 74% plan deployment within two years. The report explicitly raises 'the specter of unintended risks' from this gap.

PRODUCTION DEPLOYMENT COMPLEXITY: The report describes a systematic mismatch between pilot and production requirements—pilots use clean data, small teams, and isolated environments, while production exposes edge cases, demands system integrations, compliance reviews, and ongoing maintenance. This creates a 'vicious cycle' where companies keep funding new low-risk pilots rather than scaling proven ones.

INFRASTRUCTURE AND DATA READINESS DECLINING RELATIVE TO STRATEGY CONFIDENCE: Perceptions of preparedness shifted downward for technical infrastructure, data management, and talent—meaning the operational foundations required to support AI at scale are not keeping pace with strategic ambition.

REVENUE GROWTH STILL ASPIRATIONAL: Only 20% of organizations are currently growing revenue through AI, while 74% hope to do so in the future—suggesting the most commercially significant AI benefit remains largely unrealized across the enterprise landscape.

CLOSE THE ACCESS-ACTIVATION GAP: The report recommends moving beyond simply provisioning AI tool access to actively ensuring employees are using those tools in daily workflows—treating utilization, not just access, as the key metric.

UNLOCK HUMAN ADVANTAGE: Leading organizations are described as rethinking how work gets done so that human strengths—judgment, creativity, empathy, relationship-building—are elevated rather than automated, redesigning operating models with AI rather than just layering AI on top of existing ones.

BUILD GOVERNANCE BEFORE SCALE: The report explicitly recommends establishing oversight frameworks, accountability structures, and control mechanisms for autonomous agents before widespread agentic AI deployment—not after.

ADDRESS SOVEREIGN AI REQUIREMENTS PROACTIVELY: Given that 77% of companies already factor AI development location into procurement decisions, the report recommends leaders develop an explicit sovereign AI position covering where models are built, hosted, and governed.

BUILD A 'LIVING' TECHNOLOGY AND DATA INFRASTRUCTURE: Rather than one-time infrastructure upgrades, the report calls for a continuously evolving technology and data foundation capable of supporting AI at scale and adapting as AI capabilities evolve.

PURSUE STRATEGIC REINVENTION, NOT JUST EFFICIENCY OPTIMIZATION: The report distinguishes between companies optimizing existing processes (majority) versus those reimagining business models, offerings, roles, and ways of working (34%). It positions the latter as the only path to lasting competitive differentiation.

ESTABLISH CLEAR PILOT-TO-PRODUCTION ROADMAPS: Upfront definition of what success looks like at production scale—including infrastructure, compliance, integration, and ROI criteria—is identified as essential to avoid 'pilot fatigue' and the proof-of-concept trap.

MANUFACTURING, LOGISTICS, AND DEFENSE — PHYSICAL AI: These sectors are cited as leading global Physical AI adoption, integrating robotics, autonomous vehicles, and drones into core operations. The report notes 58% of companies across sectors are already using Physical AI to some degree.

ASIA PACIFIC MARKETS — PHYSICAL AI ADOPTION LEADERSHIP: Asia Pacific markets are specifically cited as driving Physical AI adoption and 'setting the pace for the next wave of industrial automation,' making this a relevant use case context for organizations operating in or competing with that region.

HEALTHCARE — AI STRATEGY AND PILOT SCALING: A healthcare AI leader is interviewed about the challenge of moving from pilots to production at scale, illustrating that healthcare is an active domain for enterprise AI scaling efforts with specific governance and compliance complexity.

ENTERPRISE-WIDE AGENTIC AI DEPLOYMENT: 74% of companies across industries plan to deploy autonomous AI agents within two years for enterprise operations, representing a broad cross-sector use case that is imminent rather than speculative.

FINANCIAL SERVICES, LIFE SCIENCES, TECH/MEDIA/TELECOM, CONSUMER, ENERGY/INDUSTRIALS, GOVERNMENT/PUBLIC SERVICES: These are the six industries surveyed, all of which are represented in the findings, though the report does not provide industry-specific breakdowns for most key statistics in the sections available.

whats_ahead.content: The report projects several near-term and medium-term developments: (1) MASSIVE PRODUCTION SCALE-UP IMMINENT: 54% of organizations expect to move 40%+ of AI experiments into production within the next 3–6 months, suggesting a significant wave of enterprise AI deployment is pending. (2) PHYSICAL AI NEAR-UNIVERSAL ADOPTION: Physical AI adoption is projected to reach 80% of companies within two years, up from 58% today. (3) AGENTIC AI MAINSTREAM DEPLOYMENT: Nearly 3 in 4 companies plan agentic AI deployment within two years, raising urgent governance build-out requirements. (4) SOVEREIGN AI AS ONGOING STRATEGIC CONSTRAINT: Geographic considerations in AI development and procurement are expected to remain a permanent strategic factor rather than a transient geopolitical concern. (5) THE COMPETITIVE BIFURCATION: The report implies a widening gap between the 34% of organizations pursuing genuine business reimagination through AI and the majority optimizing at the margins—suggesting market concentration of AI-driven competitive advantage in the hands of a smaller set of early transformers. The report frames the broader trajectory as AI expanding 'beyond the digital core' into physical operations and autonomous action, demanding that organizational strategy, governance, and human oversight mechanisms evolve at the same pace as the technology itself.
